item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image cannot be sourced and the second image has no source found under Creative Commons; as such, it is assumed these images are non-compliant. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Similarly. Too much conceptual overlap with the tooth fairies for me to enjoy it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh that's… creepy.
I know it's obvious but that's what it is.
It's also disgusting.

It also sucks to think about it.

Like does it effect internal body parts? Will it blocks veins? intestines? How painful is it to have teeth growing in the vagina and urethra…

I need sleep, now I can't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh well, that's okay then. I was expecting some kind of jump scare photo.
*scrolls down*
GAH!! The teeth!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is a lot better than SCP-478, and much better written. It also freaks me out a lot. I haven't had that kind of reaction to an SCP since 835. I was pretty shocked that it was at 0 before I came along. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback, guys! Nice to see this climb into the positives!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, and the fact that I had annoying gum pain a few days ago only adds to the impact of the article.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't feel that "we already have one of those" is necessarily reason for a down vote. I feel this article stands in its own, is creepy and squicked me out, but wad also interesting and maintained good pacing, tone, and clinical text. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While this may seem to use a couple ideas from other SCP pages, I found it very well written, and deserving of good ratings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this well written enough to stand on its own, even given the obvious similarities. It does make me wonder why anomalous teeth are such a popular subject for skips, though. what is it about teeth that makes them so creepy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First time I've logged on to comment and upvote in ages. Must say it's almost entirely because of the 'how it was discovered bit' and that you pulled that off very well in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. Its well written and intelligent. Although the article is different enough from other articles, there is enough similarities that I feel like they could all be connected somehow, but also stand alone if need be…if that makes any sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like the sub-anomaly, and I like the thoroughness of the teething process. Makes me feel like chewing something reading about it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The quality of the writing isn't bad. Unfortunately, though no fault of your own, the fact that it's following a very similar skip pretty closely on the mainlist affects my vote. I also found the end section with the sub-anomaly to be an odd and dissonant note to the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is different enough from kaktus's article that it can stand on it's own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually thought this was a rewrite of the other one… and was wondering -why-, if the first one was quite good o.o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I hadn't read yours, but having read it now… yeah. Wow. I actually prefer your version, too. Maybe I should just delete this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having also written an article recently about teeth, I understand how difficult it can be to try and distance yourself from the tooth fairies. I'm afraid, though, that I see more similarities between this and mine, than this and the tooth fairies, and am conflicted as to how I should feel about it.

Unfortunately, I personally just cannot make out enough differences to provide an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel I should make a brief comment about 478 - while I had read it prior to writing this, it was a long time ago, and I'd forgotten all about it until someone pointed out on the forum that this is similar. I realise they are similar, and that -1 is almost a reverse Tothbrush, but it's not plagiarism, and, as such, I think it can stand. It seems to me reasonable to assume that in the SCP universe, there will be many distinct anomalies that do similar things, as there in the real world animals that end up similar via filling the same evolutionary niche even if they are entirely distinct taxonomically.

But as this is a creative writing place, I totally respect your decision to downvote this for lack of uniqueness.

EDIT: and this post gave me my fourth pip. Wooo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Different enough from that other teeth-growing skip to be worthy of an upvote. However, it is borderline anomalous… I mean, this is basically an infectious growth that happens to be teeth, bypassing epithelial regeneration and forming new dental tissue instead. Teratomas, for instance, are tumors capable of growing teeth (and skin, and hair) inside the ovary. With an identified infectious agent, we would have to admit this is sort of -EX.

The story is inexplicably driving my hand to the + button, though. So… yeah, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although this is well-written, it reminds me far too much of SCP-478, and that bit at the end immediately makes me think of SCP-063.

Reluctant downvote.

item.title: New SCP
item.content:encoded:
It's been a while since I last wrote an SCP, but I got the inspiration for this from the weird cases of hyperdontia that have made the news recently (this and this) and the problems I've had with my own teeth recently (not hyperdontia, I'm pleased to say!) which made me think about how inherently creepy teeth are.

Thanks to the people on the forum who gave me feedback.

Bonus points to anyone who spots the link to one of my other works.
